front: What is the definition of two sets having the same cardinality?
back: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if there exists a bijective function (a one-to-one and onto function) from A to B. This is denoted as |A| = |B|.

A set is countable if it is finite or if it has the same cardinality as the set of natural numbers ℕ (i.e., it can be put into an infinite sequence). An infinite countable set is said to be countably infinite.

front: What is the cardinality of the power set of a set?
back: Cantor's Theorem states that for any set S, the cardinality of its power set P(S) is strictly greater than the cardinality of S itself (|P(S)| > |S|). This shows there are infinitely many levels of infinity.

front: What does it mean for a set to be uncountable?
back: A set is uncountable if it is infinite but does not have the same cardinality as the set of natural numbers ℕ. Its cardinality is strictly greater than |ℕ|. The set of all real numbers ℝ is a classic example of an uncountable set.

The continuum hypothesis states that there is no set whose cardinality is strictly between that of the integers (ℵ₀) and the real numbers (c). It proposes that |ℝ| = ℵ₁, the next cardinal number after ℵ₀. It was shown to be independent of the standard ZFC axioms of set theory.
